name: master stuffed poblanos
instructions:
THREE VARIANTS. All use cream cheese as the binding fat — non-negotiable across the family. Variant A is american/mexican-leaning with seeds; variants B and C are both white-bean-based, differing on whether they use from-scratch indian SPICES (B) or a shortcut massaman curry paste (C).

SHARED PREP:
- Preheat oven to 400°F.
- Prep the poblanos: HALVE lengthwise and seed them, OR stuff whole with the stem/top cut off — use judgement based on the specific poblanos (see note below). Don't parboil.
- Start the poblanos baking while you make the filling.

=== VARIANT A — CREAM CHEESE AND SEEDS (AMERICAN / MEXICAN-LEANING) ===

- Toast a mix of pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds, and/or sesame seeds in vegetable oil with minced garlic until fragrant.
- Add chia seeds off-heat (they don't need toasting).
- Stir in garlic powder, onion powder, salt, and black pepper. Paprika or oregano if you want more mexican lean.
- Off heat, fold in cream cheese until the mixture comes together as a stuffable filling.
- Stuff the poblanos, return to the oven, finish until the cheese is bubbling and the poblano skin is blistered.

=== VARIANT B — WHITE BEAN + INDIAN SPICES (FROM-SCRATCH SPICE WORK) ===

Priya Krishna-adjacent. Uses actual indian spices bloomed in oil instead of a shortcut paste.

- Bloom whole spices in vegetable oil over medium heat: whole cardamom, cinnamon stick (broken), whole cloves, fennel seeds. Just briefly, until fragrant.
- Add minced garlic (non-negotiable in variant B — see notes) and cook another minute.
- Add ground spices: cumin, coriander, turmeric, and GARAM MASALA (non-negotiable). Toast in the oil.
- No fresh onion at any stage — see note below.
- Add white beans and cook, mashing them as you go. Add splashes of milk as needed to keep the bottom from burning while you mash and cook the funk out of the beans.
- Season with salt to taste (unlike variant C, this variant has no pre-salted curry paste doing that work).
- When the bean paste tastes done (smooth, no raw-bean character), fold in cream cheese to taste — 1/8 to 1/4 of a block, or two large spoonfuls. Don't be too delicate.
- Stuff the poblanos, return to the oven, finish until done. Some bean paste will leak out — that's fine.

=== VARIANT C — WHITE BEAN + MASSAMAN CURRY PASTE (SHORTCUT) ===

Same filling shape as variant B but uses a store-bought massaman curry paste as the spice base. Fewer steps; the paste does most of the spice work in one jar.

- Warm the massaman curry paste in vegetable oil until fragrant. GARLIC IS OPTIONAL HERE (see notes) — massaman paste has shrimp paste and other umami-carriers doing the aromatic-savory work garlic would otherwise do. Add garlic if you want (a clove or two), skip it if the paste is doing enough on its own.
- Add white beans and cook, mashing as you go. Add splashes of milk to keep the bottom from burning.
- DON'T ADD SALT — massaman paste is usually salty enough on its own.
- No fresh onion at any stage.
- Fold in cream cheese to taste — 1/8 to 1/4 of a block.
- Stuff the poblanos, return to the oven, finish until done.
notes:
THE CORE — WHAT MAKES A STUFFED POBLANO A STUFFED POBLANO: after working through the variants above, the actual structural core of this dish comes down to FOUR THINGS that would be true across any future variant:
- CHEESE (ESPECIALLY CREAM CHEESE): the binding fat. Cream cheese is canonical in every variant so far; another dairy cheese could substitute (see variant A's mexican-lean cheese notes), but SOME cheese is doing the binder work.
- LIME: the acid finish. Non-negotiable across every variant (see note below).
- POBLANO: the pepper vessel. This is the whole recipe family's namesake — jalapeño, bell pepper, cubanelle etc. give you a different dish. See 'why poblano, not jalapeño' below.
- A STARCH (BEANS OR POTATOES): the filling body. White beans in variants B and C; seeds fill this slot in variant A (seed-mass acts as the starch-body substitute); potatoes in the want-to-try variants below.
EVERYTHING ELSE IS VARIANT-DEFINING RATHER THAN DISH-DEFINING: spices, garlic, cilantro, herbs, seeds all sit in secondary tier at the master level even though many are non-negotiable WITHIN a specific variant (garlic is non-negotiable in variant B, seeds are the whole point of variant A, etc). The distinction: variant-necessary vs family-necessary. This CORE framework is what you'd carry forward to design a new variant (see WANT TO TRY below for potato-based ideas) — nail cheese + lime + poblano + starch and you can rebuild the surrounding flavor stack however you want.

LIME IS NON-NEGOTIABLE ACROSS ALL VARIANTS — THIS DISH IS WHERE LIME SHINES: squeeze fresh lime over the stuffed poblanos at the plate (or into the filling at the very end for the bean-based variants B and C). Non-optional in all three variants. The cream cheese + roasted poblano + spiced filling combination all read RICHER, ROUNDER, and MORE INTEGRATED when the lime hits — the acid cuts the dairy and lifts the pepper's smoky-grassy character without competing with anything. This is genuinely one of the recipes in the cookbook where lime is doing structural work rather than just brightening. Don't substitute lemon — lemon's rounder acid reads wrong against the poblano's specific character; the citrus-forward, slightly floral edge of lime is what pairs.

CILANTRO IS NICE-TO-HAVE ACROSS ALL VARIANTS: a scatter of fresh chopped cilantro on top at serving works in all three variants. Not required, but welcome — the fresh herb + lime + cream cheese combination reads noticeably fresher and more finished. Skip if you don't have it or if you're a cilantro-tastes-like-soap person; the dish holds up without.

POBLANO IS THE CENTER OF GRAVITY — DAIRY IS THE BINDER: across all three variants, the poblano is the dish's structural and flavor anchor. The filling exists to bridge the pepper's smoky-grassy character with a creamy fat. That binder is cream cheese in every variant — no exceptions, no substitutions. Coconut milk doesn't work here, even in the massaman variant where it would normally pair beautifully; the poblano wants dairy, not coconut.

HALVE OR STUFF WHOLE — USE JUDGEMENT BASED ON THE POBLANO: previously this recipe insisted on halving lengthwise and never stuffing whole. That rule is out. Poblanos have a weird topology — some are curvy but flat, and halving them makes each half read as flat rather than a proper boat. Others are rounder and take the halve-lengthwise treatment beautifully. Use judgement based on the specific poblanos you have:
- HALVE LENGTHWISE: works for poblanos that are rounded or have enough depth to hold filling in the cut halves. Presents cleanly on a plate.
- STUFF WHOLE: cut off the top/stem, seed through the opening, and pack filling in from the top. Better for flat-shaped poblanos where halving would produce two flat pieces. Slightly messier but works for the geometry you actually have.
The halve rule works for smaller thicker-walled peppers (jalapeño-shaped) where they naturally form boats. Poblanos are more variable.

NEVER PARBOIL THE POBLANOS — SOG FACTOR: stuffed bell peppers and stuffed onions sometimes get parboiled to soften before the bake. Don't do that with poblanos. Poblanos get soggy faster than the thicker-walled bell peppers and onions; parboiling collapses their structure and you end up with limp pepper skins around the filling instead of a roasted, blistered shell. Roast them dry from the start.

WHY POBLANO, NOT JALAPEÑO: poblanos are the right pepper for this dish format — wall thickness for stuffing, heat low enough to eat as a main, and a smoky-grassy character that bridges with dairy. Baked jalapeños (the obvious alternative for a stuffed-pepper dish) are unpleasant: they're too spicy as a main, and the act of baking them releases capsaicin and pepper aerosols that irritate eyes and lungs. Not worth the kitchen suffering. Poblanos give you the same stuffed-pepper format without that downside.

=== VARIANT A NOTES ===

- SEEDS ARE THE PROTEIN: pumpkin seeds (pepitas) are canonical; sunflower seeds and sesame seeds also work. Chia seeds add a subtle thickening to the cream cheese binder. Mix freely — no rigid axis here.
- OCCIDENTAL SPICE BASE: garlic powder and onion powder are the workhorses. New mexican chili powder, paprika, and dried oregano lean it more mexican; black pepper and salt are non-negotiable.
- TOAST THE SEEDS FIRST: raw seeds in the filling read flat. Toasting in oil with garlic before folding in the cream cheese builds the savory depth this variant needs since it doesn't have curry paste or indian spices doing that work.
- OPTIONAL HIGHER-EFFORT PATH — PRE-ROAST AND PEEL: roast the poblano halves first, peel off the charred skin, THEN stuff with the filling and finish-roast. Smoother texture, deeper poblano flavor. More work, but the result is noticeably cleaner than the dry-roast-then-stuff path.
- PAIRS WITH WESTERN SALMON DISHES: the cream cheese filling gives this variant a natural pairing with western salmon preparations (french leek-and-cream salmon, lemon-butter salmon, gravlax-adjacent). The cream cheese in both the salmon plate and inside the pepper anchors the meal around the same fat profile without competing.

=== VARIANT B NOTES (INDIAN SPICES) ===

- BLOOM WHOLE SPICES FIRST: variant B does actual from-scratch indian spice work — whole cardamom, cinnamon stick, whole cloves, and fennel seeds bloomed in oil before the ground spices go in. This is the technique that makes B different from C (which shortcuts this with a paste). Skip the whole-spice bloom and B collapses toward a generic-indian filling.
- GROUND SPICE MIX: cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala are the ground-spice base. Add after the whole-spice bloom and before the beans go in. Garam masala is non-negotiable — it's the ground-spice glue that ties the whole-spice bloom to the beans.
- GARLIC IS NON-NEGOTIABLE: variant B needs garlic to build the aromatic-savory base. Unlike variant C (which gets savory-umami depth from the massaman paste's shrimp paste and other components), variant B has to build that slot from scratch — garlic is doing that work.
- NO FRESH ONION: onion doesn't belong in either variant B or C. The white-bean + cream cheese + indian-spice combination reads clean and integrated without onion; adding raw or sautéed onion introduces a texture and sweetness that fights the smooth mash-and-spice filling. Garlic carries the allium slot alone.
- SALT TO TASTE: unlike variant C (massaman paste), variant B has no pre-salted curry paste doing salt work — you need to salt the filling yourself.

=== VARIANT C NOTES (MASSAMAN CURRY PASTE) ===

- MASSAMAN PASTE IS THE 'INDIAN THING ON THE THAI SHELF': massaman paste is essentially indian in spice profile (cumin, coriander, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves) despite being a 'thai yellow curry paste.' It's the most indian thing on the thai curry shelf, and one jar does most of the spice work variant B does from scratch. Original priya krishna recipe uses this.
- GARLIC IS OPTIONAL — MASSAMAN HAS SHRIMP PASTE / UMAMI ALREADY: unlike variant B (where garlic is non-negotiable), variant C's massaman paste contains shrimp paste (or a vegan analog) and other umami-carriers that do the aromatic-savory work garlic would otherwise contribute. You can add a clove or two of garlic if you want; the dish holds up fine without it. This is a shortcut characteristic — the paste is a compressed flavor package that includes what garlic would normally add.
- DON'T ADD SALT: massaman paste is usually salty enough on its own. Taste before adding any.
- NO FRESH ONION HERE EITHER: same rule as variant B. The paste + white bean + cream cheese combination reads clean and integrated without onion.

=== SHARED VARIANT B/C NOTES ===

- WHITE BEANS, NOT CHICKPEAS OR LENTILS: white beans mash into a smooth filling-shape; chickpeas stay too discrete and lentils go too loose. Stick with white beans here.
- MILK FOR DEGLAZING THE MASH: small splashes of milk during the mash keep the bottom from burning and help cook the funk out of the beans. The dairy slot is shared with the cream cheese — they're both binding the pepper to the filling.
- B AND C ARE INTERCHANGEABLE BUT DIFFERENT IN CHARACTER: variant B (from-scratch spices) gives you a slightly more layered, distinctly-indian filling with the whole-spice bloom carrying subtle floral character. Variant C (massaman paste) is faster and more consistent but reads slightly more thai/southeast-asian at the edges. Pick B when you have the whole spices and want to lean indian; pick C when you have the jar and want dinner in 30 minutes.

=== WANT TO TRY — POTATOES + POSSIBLE FUTURE VARIANTS ===

POTATOES IN STUFFED POBLANOS — WANT TO TRY: I want to test potatoes as a filling component at some point. Two potential paths worth exploring:

- VARIANT D (POTENTIAL) — MEXICAN, WITH POTATOES AND RED CHILI FLAVORS: a mexican-shaped filling built on parboiled potatoes + red chile paste (guajillo/ancho/new mexican — chile-colorado-adjacent) + cream cheese binder. The potato-and-red-chile combination reads distinctly mexican and would give the master a proper mexican variant (currently variant A is american/mexican-leaning but not really mexican; B and C are indian/thai). This would use the same halve-or-stuff-whole judgement as B and C.

- VARIANT C EXTENSION — BEANS + POTATOES + DIFFERENT CURRY PASTES: extend variant C's paste-shortcut model to accept a combination of beans AND potatoes together, with different thai curry pastes (green, red, yellow, panang) as the aromatic base. The paste-shortcut structure is genuinely flexible; mixing white beans and parboiled potatoes in the filling and swapping the paste variety opens up a family of thai-inflected stuffed poblanos beyond just massaman. Green and panang curry pastes would each read as their own variant.

Either direction is a real recipe expansion. The potato addition needs testing — parboiled and cubed small is my starting hypothesis, so they integrate with the bean mash rather than sitting as discrete chunks.
